From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Internment camp](/w/index.php?title=Internment_camp&redirect=no "Internment camp"))

Imprisonment or confinement of groups of people without trial

For the TV episode, see [Internment (The Walking Dead)](/wiki/Internment_(The_Walking_Dead) "Internment (The Walking Dead)"). Not to be confused with [interment](/wiki/Burial "Burial") (burial) or [extermination camp](/wiki/Extermination_camp "Extermination camp").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Boercamp1.jpg/330px-Boercamp1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boercamp1.jpg)

[Boer](/wiki/Boers "Boers") women and children in a [British concentration camp](/wiki/Second_Boer_War_concentration_camps "Second Boer War concentration camps") in South Africa (1899–1902)

**Internment** is the imprisonment of people, commonly in large groups, without [charges](/wiki/Criminal_charge "Criminal charge")[[1]](#cite_note-1) or [intent to file charges](/wiki/Indictment "Indictment").[[2]](#cite_note-2) The term is especially used for the confinement "of enemy citizens in [wartime](/wiki/War "War") or of [terrorism](/wiki/Terrorism "Terrorism") suspects".[[3]](#cite_note-3) Thus, while it can simply mean [imprisonment](/wiki/Imprisonment "Imprisonment"), it tends to refer to preventive confinement rather than confinement *after* having been convicted of some crime. Use of these terms is subject to debate and political sensitivities.[[4]](#cite_note-euph-4) The word *internment* is also occasionally used to describe a [neutral country](/wiki/Neutral_country "Neutral country")'s practice of detaining [belligerent](/wiki/Belligerent "Belligerent") [armed forces](/wiki/Military "Military") and equipment on its territory during times of war, under the [Hague Convention of 1907](/wiki/Hague_Conventions_of_1899_and_1907#Hague_Convention_of_1907 "Hague Conventions of 1899 and 1907").[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)

Interned persons may be held in [prisons](/wiki/Prison "Prison") or in facilities known as **internment camps** or **[concentration camps](/wiki/Concentration_camp "Concentration camp")**. The term *concentration camp* originates from the Spanish–Cuban [Ten Years' War](/wiki/Ten_Years%27_War "Ten Years' War") when Spanish forces detained Cuban civilians in camps in order to more easily combat guerrilla forces. Over the following decades the British during the [Second Boer War](/wiki/Second_Boer_War "Second Boer War") and the Americans during the [Philippine–American War](/wiki/Philippine%E2%80%93American_War "Philippine–American War") also used concentration camps.

The terms *concentration camp* and *internment camp* are used to refer to a variety of systems that greatly differ in their severity, mortality rate, and architecture; their defining characteristic is that inmates are held outside the [rule of law](/wiki/Rule_of_law "Rule of law").[[6]](#cite_note-6) [Extermination camps](/wiki/Extermination_camp "Extermination camp") or death camps, whose primary purpose is killing, are also imprecisely referred to as *concentration camps*.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights") restricts the use of internment, with Article 9 stating, "No one shall be subjected to [arbitrary arrest](/wiki/Arbitrary_arrest_and_detention "Arbitrary arrest and detention"), [detention](/wiki/Detention_(imprisonment) "Detention (imprisonment)") or [exile](/wiki/Exile "Exile")."[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Defining internment and concentration camp

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Fort_Marr.JPG/250px-Fort_Marr.JPG)](/wiki/File:Fort_Marr.JPG)

Fort Marr is the last surviving remnant of the American forts used to intern the [Cherokee](/wiki/Cherokee_removal "Cherokee removal") in preparation for their [removal](/wiki/Indian_removal "Indian removal") to Indian Territory, months prior the "[Trail of Tears](/wiki/Trail_of_Tears "Trail of Tears")".[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Weyler_reconcentrados.png/250px-Weyler_reconcentrados.png)](/wiki/File:Weyler_reconcentrados.png)

Cuban victims of [Spanish reconcentration policies](/wiki/Reconcentration_policy "Reconcentration policy"), 1896.

The *[American Heritage Dictionary](/wiki/American_Heritage_Dictionary "American Heritage Dictionary")* defines the term *concentration camp* as: "A camp where persons are confined, usually without hearings and typically under harsh conditions, often as a result of their membership in a group which the government has identified as dangerous or undesirable."[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Although the first example of civilian internment may date as far back as the 1830s,[[11]](#cite_note-11) the English term *concentration camp* was first used in order to refer to the [reconcentration camps](/wiki/Reconcentration_policy "Reconcentration policy") (Spanish:*reconcentrados*) which were set up by the [Spanish military](/wiki/Spain_under_the_Restoration "Spain under the Restoration") in [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba") during the [Ten Years' War](/wiki/Ten_Years%27_War "Ten Years' War") (1868–1878).[[12]](#cite_note-Columbia-12)[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13) The label was applied yet again to camps set up by the United States during the [Philippine–American War](/wiki/Philippine%E2%80%93American_War "Philippine–American War") (1899–1902).[[14]](#cite_note-14) And expanded usage of the *concentration camp* label continued, when the [British set up camps](/wiki/Second_Boer_War_concentration_camps "Second Boer War concentration camps") during the [Second Boer War](/wiki/Second_Boer_War "Second Boer War") (1899–1902) in South Africa for interning [Boers](/wiki/Boer "Boer") during the same time period.[[12]](#cite_note-Columbia-12)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

During the 20th century, the arbitrary internment of civilians by the state reached its most extreme forms in the [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") [Gulag system of concentration camps](/wiki/List_of_Gulag_camps "List of Gulag camps") (1918–1991)[[16]](#cite_note-:1-16) and the [Nazi concentration camps](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camps "Nazi concentration camps") (1933–1945). The Soviet system was the first applied by a government on its own citizens.[[13]](#cite_note-:0-13) The Gulag consisted in over 30,000 camps for most of its existence (1918–1991) and detained some 18 million from 1929 until 1953,[[16]](#cite_note-:1-16) which is only a third of its 73-year lifespan. The Nazi concentration camp system was extensive, with as many as 15,000 camps[[17]](#cite_note-jewishvirtuallibrary1-17) and at least 715,000 simultaneous internees.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The total number of casualties in these camps is difficult to determine, but the deliberate policy of [extermination through labor](/wiki/Extermination_through_labor "Extermination through labor") in many of the camps was designed to ensure that the inmates would die of starvation, untreated disease and [summary executions](/wiki/Summary_execution "Summary execution") within set periods of time.[[19]](#cite_note-Marek_Przybyszewski-19) Moreover, Nazi Germany established six [extermination camps](/wiki/Extermination_camp "Extermination camp"), specifically designed to kill millions of people, primarily by [gassing](/wiki/Extermination_camp#Gassing "Extermination camp").[[20]](#cite_note-Gellately2001-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Buchenwald_Slave_Laborers_Liberation.jpg/250px-Buchenwald_Slave_Laborers_Liberation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Buchenwald_Slave_Laborers_Liberation.jpg)

Jewish slave laborers at the [Buchenwald concentration camp](/wiki/Buchenwald_concentration_camp "Buchenwald concentration camp") near [Weimar](/wiki/Weimar "Weimar") photographed after their liberation by the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") on 16 April 1945

As a result, the term "concentration camp" is sometimes conflated with the concept of an "[extermination camp](/wiki/Extermination_camp "Extermination camp")" and historians debate whether the term "concentration camp" or the term "internment camp" should be used to describe other examples of civilian internment.[[4]](#cite_note-euph-4)

The "concentration camp" label continues to see expanded use for cases post-[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), for instance in relation to [British camps in Kenya](/wiki/List_of_British_detention_camps_during_the_Mau_Mau_Uprising "List of British detention camps during the Mau Mau Uprising") during the [Mau Mau rebellion](/wiki/Mau_Mau_rebellion "Mau Mau rebellion") (1952–1960),[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) and camps set up in [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile") during the [military dictatorship](/wiki/Military_dictatorship "Military dictatorship") of [Augusto Pinochet](/wiki/Augusto_Pinochet "Augusto Pinochet") (1973–1990).[[24]](#cite_note-24) According to the [United States Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense") as many as 3 million [Uyghurs](/wiki/Uyghurs "Uyghurs") and members of other [Muslim](/wiki/Islam_in_China "Islam in China") minority groups are being held in [China](/wiki/China "China")'s [re-education camps](/wiki/Xinjiang_re-education_camps "Xinjiang re-education camps") which are located in the [Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang") region and which American news reports often label as *concentration camps*.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) The camps were established in the late 2010s under [Chinese Communist Party general secretary](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party") [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping")'s [administration](/wiki/China_under_Xi_Jinping "China under Xi Jinping").[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Impact

Scholars have debated the efficacy of internment as a [counterinsurgency](/wiki/Counterinsurgency "Counterinsurgency") tactic. A 2023 study found that internment during the [Irish war of independence](/wiki/Irish_War_of_Independence "Irish War of Independence") led to greater grievances among Irish rebels and led them to fight longer in the war.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Examples

Main article: [List of concentration and internment camps](/wiki/List_of_concentration_and_internment_camps "List of concentration and internment camps")

### Active

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Xinjiang_Internment_Map%2C_US-Aus_Gov_Assessment.jpg/250px-Xinjiang_Internment_Map%2C_US-Aus_Gov_Assessment.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xinjiang_Internment_Map,_US-Aus_Gov_Assessment.jpg)

Map of the [Xinjiang internment camps](/wiki/Xinjiang_internment_camps "Xinjiang internment camps") in China based on data collected by the US [National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency](/wiki/National_Geospatial-Intelligence_Agency "National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency") and the [Australian Strategic Policy Institute](/wiki/Australian_Strategic_Policy_Institute "Australian Strategic Policy Institute")

* [North Korean prison camps](/wiki/Prisons_in_North_Korea#Internment_camps_for_political_prisoners "Prisons in North Korea") (1948–present)[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)
* [Guantanamo Bay detention camp](/wiki/Guantanamo_Bay_detention_camp "Guantanamo Bay detention camp") (2002–present)[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)
* [Refugee detention centres](/wiki/Slavery_in_Libya#Slavery_in_the_post-Gaddafi_era "Slavery in Libya") in Libya (2011–present)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38)
* [Uyghur re-education camps](/wiki/Xinjiang_re-education_camps "Xinjiang re-education camps") in China (2017–present)[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

## Combatants

Internment also refers to the detainment of combatants and equipment of a belligerent by a neutral state.[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5) During a conflict, combatants of a belligerent state that enter the territory of a neutral state should be either expelled or interned. When interned, the neutral state is responsible for ensuring the well-being of the combatants as well as ensuring they or their equipment cannot participate in the conflict until hostilities cease. [[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-:3-68)

The neutral state can hold interned personal in camps or other secure places and, at the discretion of the neutral State may be given parole but cannot be allowed to leave neutral territory. The internees must be fed and clothed, and allowed relief supplies. The belligerent State is responsible at the end of hostilities for covering the costs incurred by the neutral state. The conditions the internees are held in should be based on an agreement between the neutral State and the belligerent State, but if there is no agreement, they must be at the minimum treatment equivalent to that of POWs under the Third Geneva Convention.[[68]](#cite_note-:3-68)

If the neutral state is unable or unwilling to carry out internment or expulsion of belligerent forces within its territory the adverse party is entitled to undertake hot pursuit and attack the belligerent forces within neutral territory and seek compensation from the neutral State for a breach of neutrality. [[68]](#cite_note-:3-68)
